author: mark
text:
Session corrections to fold into next spec revision (description body is pre-corrections):

- §3 state schema: drop the closed enum {open|claimed|review}. `state` is an open string field. Only hardcoded rejection: claim refuses if already claimed. All other sequence enforcement is plugin pre-invoke. Destination semantics, not source.
- §1 layout: rename `change-attempts/` → `changes/` throughout. Move worktrees from `plugins/tracker/<remote>/change-attempts/` to `clones/<enc-local>/changes/<uuid>/`. Worktree is OFF operating/, not under tracker. Base balls is unaware whether operating/ is real-local (stealth) or symlinked (tracker).
- §6 plugin invocation: run-parts style. Per-op directories `config/plugins/<op>.{pre,post}/` containing numbered symlinks (`50-tracker`). Numeric prefix is run order. Executed sequentially.
- §8 op lifecycle: reorganize into five phases — (A) gather/hypothesize → (B) plugins pre-invoke → (C) implement diff → (D) plugins post-invoke (incl. tracker push) → (E) cleanup. CRITICAL: pre-invoke runs BEFORE the worktree is created.
- §8 failure order: rollback prior plugins (reverse) FIRST, THEN discard worktree. Not the other way.
- §5 commit message: subject defaults to the ball's `title` field verbatim. `bl-id` and `bl-op` live in the body k:v block ONLY. Reader of git log doesn't know task ids. Skill note: agents should override subject with -m when title isn't the right delivery message.
- §3 terminal ops: close/drop delete the ball file. Recovery via `git log --diff-filter=D -- tasks/<id>.md`. Cf. [[bl-close-remote-coupling]] — the question of whether close also ships code is open.
- Worktree is CORE balls, not a plugin. The earlier "code-worktree-as-plugin" option (b) was rejected. Default location XDG, overridable.

Treat these notes as authoritative until the description sections are rewritten.

author: Zeroed
text:
§5 RESOLVED (topic bl-1e4d). Fold into description on next spec revision.

The commit-message k:v block IS a standard git trailer block — last blank-line-separated paragraph, each line `key: value`, parsed via git's trailer grammar (git interpret-trailers --parse). No hand-rolled parser; inherits existing tooling. Coexists with Co-Authored-By: and other trailers.

Message shape (flips earlier subject/kv/body -> subject/body/trailers):
  <subject: defaults to ball title; -m overrides>
  <free-form body, optional>
  bl-protocol: 1
  bl-op: close
  bl-id: bl-1234
  bl-actor: orionriver@gmail.com
  bl-from-state: claimed
  bl-to-state: closed

- Grammar: tokens lower-kebab [a-z0-9-] (== git trailer token grammar). Block is the last paragraph; body above it.
- Namespacing: every key namespaced by owner. Core = bl-; `bl-` is RESERVED (plugins may not emit bl-*). Plugins prefix with own name (jira-id, github-url). balls acts only on bl-* it knows.
- Keys balls writes: always bl-protocol, bl-op, bl-actor. bl-id on every op EXCEPT init and config (no ball). bl-from-state/bl-to-state on state transitions only (omitted on create/init/config).
- Repeated keys -> list (git-native). No comma-splitting. No multi-ball MUTATING op (one ball, one commit); repeated-key lists are a non-mutating read form only.
- Unknown keys: never dropped. Parsed into metadata, balls acts only on recognized bl-*, full map forwarded to plugins on invoke wire (§7).
- Supersedes §5 stub unprefixed reserved keys (op/ball/actor/from-state/to-state/protocol) -> bl-prefixed; ball -> bl-id.

author: mark
text:
§6 RESOLVED (from bl-8452 — plugin dispatcher contract). Fold into next spec revision; authoritative until §6 is rewritten.

1. Dispatch — subprocess-uniform. Every plugin is an independent binary invoked across a process boundary; no in-process path, no privileged plugins. The shipped `tracker` is a FULLY separate binary (in-repo only because it's a default capability + the reference implementation for plugin authors), invoked identically to any third-party plugin. `bl plugin <name> <verb>` is the canonical dispatch and balls dogfoods it: balls builds the same argv + stdin payload a third party would get and execs.

2. A plugin is ONLY a binary, self-describing via `<plugin> protocol`. No install-time config, no metadata file, no `plugins:` list. `<plugin> protocol` dumps structured JSON to stdout: { protocol: <version(s)>, ops: [...] } — protocol version(s) it supports + the ops it handles. balls re-queries it live when needed (install validation, doctor); never persists it. Verb name is `protocol` (not `spec`).

3. The REGISTRY IS THE FILESYSTEM. Symlinks under `config/plugins/<op>.{pre,post}/` on the active balls branch are the single source of truth for wiring. Symlink present = run this plugin in this op-phase; numeric prefix = run order (run-parts style: sequential, blocking, non-zero aborts + reverse rollback). The old {name,path} `plugins` config field is GONE (drop it from §4 built-in fields). Default tracker is wired by `bl init` laying its symlinks, not by a config default.

4. Two-level symlink stitch (the portability solution):
   - COMMITTED (travels with the balls branch): `config/plugins/<op>.<phase>/NN-<name>` is a RELATIVE symlink -> `../bin/<name>`. Relative + internal to the checkout, so the blob is a fixed string valid in BOTH stealth and federation regardless of where the checkout physically sits. (An *escaping* link breaks: federation puts the checkout at balls/plugins/tracker/<remote>/ = 5 `..` to balls/plugins/, stealth at balls/clones/<enc>/operating/ = 6 `..`. Internal links are immune to that off-by-one.)
   - LOCAL (never committed): `config/plugins/bin/<name>` is an ABSOLUTE symlink -> this machine's actual binary. balls is the only writer to the balls branch and never stages `bin/` (gitignore it too, belt-and-suspenders). Only machine-specific artifact; doubles as the run-parts link target so 'exec everything in the folder' works literally.
   - Missing plugin = dangling `bin/<name>` = clean 'plugin <name> referenced but not installed here' error. No silent breakage.

5. `bl plugin install <plugin> <path> <ops...>` — two effects:
   - Local: create/refresh `bin/<name>` -> <path> (the stitch + persistence; survives across runs).
   - Committed: create `<op>.<phase>/NN-<name>` -> ../bin/<name> for each requested op, then commit. Default usage links every supported op: `bl plugin install jira /path/balls-jira $(balls-jira protocol | jq -r '.ops[]')`; pass a subset to wire only part of a plugin's behavior. Order prefix defaults to next-free (append); --order N to place it.
   - VALIDATION: each requested op checked against the live `<plugin> protocol`; balls refuses to link an op the plugin doesn't declare. Protocol-version check fires here too — refuse to link a plugin whose declared version set doesn't include balls' current protocol version. No runtime renegotiation; version fixed per binary.
   - Install is itself an op (commits atomically, visible in git log).
   - Write target: the ACTIVE operating repo's balls branch (federated -> the federated checkout; stealth -> the stealth repo). User-overridable.
   - Fresh clone: committed op-phase links exist but `bin/<name>` doesn't; re-run install with this box's path to re-stitch — idempotent on the already-present committed links.

6. Recursion guard — `BALLS_PLUGIN_DEPTH`, hard cap (built-in constant). balls sets it in the plugin env, increments per nesting level. At the cap, nested ops run PLUGIN-FREE (suppressed), NOT refused — so a plugin can always shell back to `bl` to read/write without cascading plugin chains. A plugin MAY deliberately override to re-enable plugins on a nested call. Applies to ALL ops, incl. reads.

7. Snapshot: §8 step 1 = the running op reads the committed `config/plugins/<op>.{pre,post}/` set at op-start and uses that frozen set; an install landing mid-op affects only the NEXT op.

§8 CONSEQUENCE — reads are no longer special-cased. DROP 'Reads (bl list, bl show) never invoke plugins.' New rule: every op (incl. show/list) has `<op>.{pre,post}/` hook dirs and runs whatever is linked there. Nothing is linked into read ops by default (tracker doesn't support them), so reads stay plugin-free IN PRACTICE, not by rule. Link a plugin into show.pre and it runs on `bl show`.

author: Binges
text:
§7 + §8 RESOLVED (topic bl-4997 — pre-invoke return channel + field-conflict policy). Fold into next spec revision; authoritative until §7/§8 are rewritten. SUPERSEDES the "CRITICAL: pre-invoke runs BEFORE the worktree is created" line in the 2026-05-30 corrections note — it now runs AFTER.

RESOLUTION BY REMOVAL: there is no pre-invoke return channel, and therefore no field-conflict policy to specify. Both questions dissolve once the change worktree is created BEFORE pre-invoke instead of after. (An earlier bl-4997 resolution that kept a stdout return channel and a strict-error conflict rule was built on a stale draft and rolled back; this is the corrected one.)

1. WORKTREE-FIRST LIFECYCLE. §8's five phases become:
   A. gather + create the change worktree (changes/<uuid>/), stage balls' proposed diff (uncommitted)
   B. pre-invoke plugins — cwd = worktree; they mutate the op by EDITING FILES, or reject via non-zero exit
   C. balls commits the worktree with the §5 trailer block; re-reads the tree to learn the final id/state
   D. post-invoke plugins — cwd = worktree; react to the committed change (tracker push, etc.)
   E. merge into operating/ + cleanup
   Failure order unchanged from the 05-30 note: rollback prior plugins (reverse) FIRST, then discard the worktree.

2. NO RETURN CHANNEL. A plugin contributes by writing the worktree (ball file, frontmatter; commit via `git commit --amend` in a post-plugin), NOT by printing field_overrides / metadata_additions for balls to merge. stdout is diagnostics only — nothing printed is parsed back into state. This deletes the field_overrides / metadata_additions protocol from the §7 draft.

3. FIELD CONFLICTS = THE FILESYSTEM. Two plugins writing the same field are two writes to a file: last writer wins, and chain order (the run-parts NN- prefixes) already determines who is last. balls neither detects, arbitrates, nor tracks ownership. No strict-error rule, no namespacing, no id-authority. (balls MAY later add an OPTIONAL diff-and-refuse hard error, but it is not load-bearing and not part of the base contract.)

4. pre vs post STILL EARN THEIR KEEP — the divider is the COMMIT boundary, not "worktree or not". pre = the record isn't sealed; you can still shape what gets committed (e.g. jira assigns the ball id). post = the commit is final; react to it (tracker needs the final hash to push). Both run in the same worktree. bl-8452's <op>.pre / <op>.post run-parts dirs are unchanged.

5. id IS AN ORDINARY FIELD. The change worktree is uuid-named, so nothing depends on knowing the id up front. balls stages tasks/<provisional-id>.md; a plugin that reassigns (jira -> PROJ-1234) does `git mv` + a frontmatter edit; balls re-reads the tree to learn the final id. NO id_authority config key, no immutability guard, no create-only check. A plugin that rewrites an id in flight owns the result.

6. OUT OF SCOPE / DISCOURAGED: a pre-plugin changing the OP KIND itself (close -> drop). balls owns op identity; not a supported mutation.

7. READS stay worktree-free in practice: balls only stages a diff for mutating ops, and nothing is linked into show.pre / list.pre by default (bl-8452). A plugin linked into a read op runs with cwd = operating/, no worktree.

SEAM WITH bl-8452 (answers bl-5dc2's check): consistent by construction. No stdout-parsed-into-state, so nothing to reconcile with subprocess-uniform dispatch; no persisted plugin list assumed (the symlink folder is the registry); 'ops' not 'verbs'; recursion (BALLS_PLUGIN_DEPTH suppress-not-refuse) unaffected — a pre-plugin that shells back to bl hits the depth guard normally.

author: Binges
text:
SEAM CONFIRMED — bl-4997 ↔ bl-8452 (topic bl-5dc2). The bl-4997 resolution (worktree-first lifecycle, NO return channel — see the "§7 + §8 RESOLVED" note above) is consistent with bl-8452's dispatcher contract by construction. Per bl-5dc2's four checks:

- Wire crossing: PASS. bl-4997 no longer has any stdout return channel; plugins mutate via the worktree (cwd). Nothing assumes an in-process call — it is subprocess-uniform like every other plugin, including tracker.
- Naming: PASS. No 'verbs'; the contract is "run in the worktree for this op-phase". Self-describe verb stays `protocol` -> { protocol, ops }.
- Field-conflict vs validation: PASS. No persisted plugin list and no metadata channel are assumed. The `plugins` config list is gone; the symlink folder is the registry; conflicts resolve in the filesystem as last-writer-wins, where "last" is the run-parts NN- order — so the registry bl-8452 defined IS the conflict-ordering mechanism. Nothing bl-8452 removed is relied upon.
- Recursion: PASS. A pre-invoke plugin that shells back to `bl` is governed by BALLS_PLUGIN_DEPTH (suppress-not-refuse); unchanged by worktree-first.

NOTE: the original concern was real — bl-4997 was first closed (concurrently, by session Binges) with a stdout return channel + strict-error conflict policy that DID drift from bl-8452. That resolution was rolled back and redone worktree-first before this confirmation. The seam holds against the corrected resolution, not the reverted one.

author: Shrubbery
text:
§9 `bl init` — RESOLVED (topic bl-6406). Supersedes the §9 draft in the description body.

VOCAB rename: route→trail, hop→step (tracker theme). Trail = anchor → step(s) → terminus, walked by following each branch's next-step pointer, stop-on-revisit (literal-string dedup; loop is the pathological-chain backstop, not the stealth mechanism). Terminus = a branch with no next-step pointer (ordinary case) or a revisit (loop). Anchor = the always-present, path-derived local `balls` branch holding this checkout's config + trail pointer. Operating = the local checkout ops physically run against; resolves to the terminus. In stealth: anchor == terminus == operating.

CORE PRINCIPLE — full symmetry, no special cases. balls core only (a) creates the anchor and (b) runs the configured plugin chain, then commits. It has ZERO knowledge of tracker/remotes/stealth. "Defaults" are install-time config: tracker ships as a compiled binary; the default plugins list = [tracker]; tracker's default remote is a plugin config. Strip tracker's references → pure local, unconfigured terminus, no code-path change. (Matches epic: balls knows a plugin's name and path, nothing else.) init is therefore a NORMAL op, not a dispatch exception: the anchor is the worktree-equivalent substrate the chain writes into; the chain is just the configured default ([tracker]) because run-parts is empty pre-init ("empty run-parts → use the default list" is the base case, not a special path).

LIFECYCLE (maps onto the normal five-phase op shape):
(A) prep — resolve handle (enc_local, clone_dir). Resolve plugin chain from config (default [tracker]; --plugins … overrides; --plugins [] = empty). Create the anchor substrate: git init, orphan `balls`, seed config/ + empty tasks/, one commit. NEVER seed empty config/plugins/<op>/<phase>/ dirs — an empty op-dir means "run nothing" and would silently suppress inheritance.
(B) init/pre — run the configured chain in order. tracker (the default):
    • received --stealth → no-op remote behavior AND write the self-reference lock on the anchor (the explicit guard so a stealth repo can't silently leak by later auto-extension); done.
    • else resolve remote: --remote > --center > XDG > `git -C <cwd> remote get-url origin`. None → E1.
    • inspect remote `balls`: absent → create orphan + push; ==1 commit → pass silently (idempotent); ≥2 → E3; unreachable → E4.
    • remote step established → write the next-step pointer into the anchor; install tracker's run-parts symlinks for future ops.
    • anchor already locked-stealth + only an auto-discovered origin → W1 (warn, do NOT auto-extend). An explicit --remote/--center is the user deliberately choosing to route onward and OVERRIDES the lock (no separate override flag).
    • any non-zero plugin exit → roll back prior plugins in reverse order, then abort (E7).
(C) implement — balls commits the anchor (seed + whatever pre wrote). init is "done."
(D) init/post — run the chain's post phase; usually a noop, available if a plugin needs it.
CONVERGING PREDICATE: operating/ ends up a valid git repo on `balls` whether the path was fresh-stealth, freshly-tracked, or re-init. Base balls cannot tell which ran — this is what makes the three indistinguishable downstream.

TWO STEALTH FORMS (same terminus rule):
• unconfigured terminus — `--plugins []` or tracker removed: no pointer written, anchor is naturally terminal, NOT locked.
• locked stealth — `--stealth`: tracker runs, no-ops its remote, writes the self-reference lock. The lock blocks AUTO-extension only; an explicit --remote/--center overrides it.

RUN-PARTS LAYOUT: config/plugins/<op>/pre/ and config/plugins/<op>/post/ (nested folders, numbered symlinks e.g. 50-tracker). Each <op>/<phase> directory is resolved INDEPENDENTLY up the trail; the first layer that defines the dir wins WHOLE — no cross-layer merge (deliberately unlike yaml list fields, which compose via _prepend/_append/_ban). Empty dir = run nothing.

ERROR / NOTICE CATALOG (verbatim strings; ownership in brackets):
E1 [tracker]: error: tracker init: no balls remote could be resolved (no --remote, no --center, no 'origin' in <cwd>). Pass --remote <url>, or 'bl init --stealth' for a local-only repo.
E2 [balls]:   error: bl init: balls is already initialized for <cwd> (anchor at <clone_dir>). Re-run with --reinit to re-run the init chain idempotently.
E3 [tracker]: error: tracker init: remote <url> already has an established 'balls' branch (<N> commits). 'init' bootstraps a fresh branch; run 'bl prime' to adopt existing remote state.
E4 [tracker]: error: tracker init: remote <url> is unreachable (<git error>). Refusing to initialize — a local 'balls' branch created now could diverge from upstream. Fix connectivity, or 'bl init --stealth'.
E7 [balls]:   error: bl init: plugin '<name>' failed during init (exit <code>); rolled back <K> prior plugin(s). See clones/<enc_local>/logs/<name>/ for details.
W1 [tracker]: warning: tracker init: the anchor for <cwd> is locked to stealth; not auto-extending it to <remote>. Pass --remote <url> explicitly to route onward.
N3 [balls]:   notice: bl init: a downstream config layer introduces plugin(s) <names> not initialized locally; deferring their init to the next 'bl prime'.

EDGE-CASE MATRIX (the six the topic named):
• Re-init on existing install → --reinit re-runs the chain idempotently; bare re-init → E2.
• No CWD repo, no --remote, tracker on → E1 (tracker can't resolve a remote).
• CWD has origin but a different balls remote is wanted → --remote overrides (--remote > origin); no error.
• --stealth (was --no-tracker) in a repo with origin → tracker no-ops the remote and sets the self-reference lock; remote ignored.
• --center=<url> → tracker writes the next-step pointer to the center (federation step); trail = anchor → center. Trail topology / config layering for multi-step federation is the federation topic's (bl-62bc) job.
• Downstream layer surfaces NEW plugins on config reread → defer their init to the next `bl prime` → N3.

OPEN / DEFERRED (not blocking §9):
• Physical anchor-vs-operating realization in tracker mode (real dir vs symlink): §1 / workspace-layout's province.
• Trail topology + config layering for multi-step federation: bl-62bc.
• E3's boundary uses the remote branch's commit count at check time (1 = the fresh seed init itself produces). If a future plugin commits during its own init, revisit whether the boundary should instead be "no tasks/* entries."

author: Shrubbery
text:
§9 ↔ §6 RECONCILIATION (corrects the "§9 RESOLVED" note above; bl-6406 follow-up).

The §9 note's "resolve plugin chain from config (default [tracker])" / "default plugins list = [tracker]" wording conflicts with §6 (bl-8452), which removed the {name,path} `plugins` config field entirely — the filesystem symlink registry IS the chain, and "default tracker is wired by bl init laying its symlinks, not by a config default."

Correct reading of §9 steps (A)/(B): there is NO persisted `plugins` config list. `bl init` lays the BUILT-IN default plugin (tracker) by installing its run-parts symlinks (the §6 `bl plugin install` mechanism); `--plugins <set>` overrides WHICH plugins init installs; `--plugins []` installs none. The chain init then runs is whatever now exists in `<op>/pre|post/` — i.e. exactly what init just laid. "Strip tracker's references" = remove tracker's symlinks.

Per-plugin config (e.g. tracker's own remote / next-step pointer) is unaffected — that's the plugin's own territory, not a balls-level plugins list. Everything else in the §9 note stands.

author: mark
text:
§ STATE TRANSITIONS (claim / unclaim / review) — RESOLVED (topic bl-d66e). Becomes a new § on next spec revision. Non-terminal, non-bootstrap ops; the everyday exercise of the §8 worktree-first lifecycle. Settled context (do not re-litigate): no state-transition validation, `state` is an open string, only hardcoded rejection is claim-on-already-`claimed` (note 0); destination semantics, not source (bl-d66e note).

THE TWO-WORKTREE SPLIT (the live question, resolved). There are TWO worktrees and they are DISTINCT core/plugin concepts on DISTINCT branch lineages — never the same git worktree (a worktree checks out one branch; §2 keeps the ball file on the `balls` orphan branch and the deliverable on a `work/<id>` lineage, so they cannot coincide without putting task state on main, which "nothing on main" forbids):

1. CHANGE worktree — `clones/<enc-local>/changes/<uuid>/`. CORE balls (note 0). Ephemeral: one per op, born in §8 phase A, torn down in phase E. Checked out to the `balls` branch of the balls-STATE repo. Stages ONLY the ball-file frontmatter diff (`tasks/<id>.md`). This is the worktree note 0 calls "core, not a plugin."

2. CODE worktree — the deliverable workspace (`.balls-worktrees/<id>/` today). Owned by a SIBLING delivery plugin, NOT base balls and NOT tracker (see OWNERSHIP). Long-lived: survives claim→review→close (three separate `bl` invocations). A `git worktree` of the PROJECT repo on branch `work/<id>`. Base balls never opens it.

OWNERSHIP — the code worktree is a SIBLING plugin (call it the delivery/worktree plugin), default-wired alongside tracker but SEPARATE from it. Rationale: you want local worktrees without a remote (stealth-but-worktrees) and a remote without worktrees to be independently expressible; bundling into tracker re-couples what this refactor separates. It reacts to DESTINATION state via §6 run-parts hooks: `claim.post` materializes, `review.post` delivers (squash/push), `close.post` tears down (the last deferred — see SCOPE). Base balls stays balls-state-only; "do something on claim/review/close against the project repo" is precisely plugin behavior.

WHY PLUGIN, NOT CORE (Option 1 of 3; the other two rejected):
- "nothing on main" becomes STRUCTURAL, not disciplinary: base balls never touches the project repo, so core CANNOT leave a commit on the integration branch. Only the delivery plugin commits there, deliberately, on `review.post`.
- review's two jobs cleanly split: (a) flip `open→review` on the balls branch = CORE §8 op on the change worktree; (b) squash `work/<id>`→integration = the `review.post` plugin. local-squash vs forge-gated stops being a core/plugin asymmetry and becomes just "which plugin is wired into review.post."
- Dominates Option 3 (drop worktrees entirely): strip the plugin and you HAVE Option 3 (claim = bare flip, portable to non-git), without losing the default worktree experience or the delivery model (`delivered_in`, gate children).
- Option 2 (code worktree in core, today's behavior) was rejected: it makes core model a second repo (project discovery, work/<id> branches, squash, merge-main conflict step), keeps the non-git/`--no-worktree` carve-outs inside core forever, and perpetuates the local-squash-in-core vs forge-as-plugin fragility. Bounded mechanism cost (Option 1) over unbounded architectural drift (Option 2).

FOUR MECHANISM COSTS Option 1 incurs (all bounded, all resolved here):
1. TWO CWDs. §6/§7 invoke plugins with cwd = the CHANGE worktree (balls-state, XDG). The delivery plugin's job is the CODE worktree (project repo, elsewhere), so it deliberately ignores its cwd and acts on the project repo — same as tracker acting on a remote. The "cwd = the thing you act on" convention does not hold for delivery; this is expected, not a bug.
2. PROJECT-REPO PATH ON THE WIRE. The plugin needs the project repo root for `git worktree add`. That path is already known to balls (the `enc-local` binding key = where `bl` was invoked); expose it as a new §7 payload field (`binding.invocation_path`). Only spec addition required.
3. WORKTREE PATH IS DERIVED, NOT RETURNED. note 3 killed the return channel (plugin stdout is diagnostics only), so the plugin cannot tell balls where it put the worktree. Resolution: the code-worktree path is a PURE FUNCTION of (binding, id). balls computes and prints it (preserving today's "claim prints the path" UX) while the plugin independently creates it at the same computed location. No return channel needed; the path is a shared convention both sides derive.
4. CROSS-OP IDEMPOTENT STATE. The change worktree is per-op; the code worktree spans three ops. The plugin owns a long-lived, id-keyed resource: `claim.post` create-if-absent, `review.post` deliver-the-existing, `close.post` remove. Stateful across ops, like tracker's clone. State lives in the plugin's own XDG territory (a plugin-owned directory; the git worktree itself may sit under `$XDG_STATE_HOME/balls/plugins/<delivery>/<enc-local>/<id>/`, bound to the project repo's `.git`).

DETERMINISTIC SEQUENCES (each is the §8 five-phase shape A–E; CORE acts only on the change worktree, the delivery PLUGIN reacts in its hooks):

claim  (open → claimed; refused if already `claimed` — the one hardcoded guard):
  A. create change worktree `changes/<uuid>/`; stage ball-file diff: `state: claimed`, `claimant: <actor>`, bump `updated` (uncommitted).
  B. `claim.pre` plugins (cwd = change worktree). e.g. a policy plugin may reject (non-zero) or edit the staged ball file.
  C. balls commits the change worktree with the §5 trailer block: `bl-op: claim`, `bl-id`, `bl-actor`, `bl-from-state: open`, `bl-to-state: claimed`. Re-reads the tree to learn final id/state.
  D. `claim.post` plugins. Delivery plugin: `git -C <project-repo> worktree add <derived-path> -b work/<id>` (create-if-absent). tracker: push the state commit if so configured.
  E. merge change worktree into `operating/`; tear down change worktree. balls prints the derived code-worktree path.

unclaim  (claimed → open):
  A. change worktree; stage `state: open`, clear `claimant`, bump `updated`.
  B. `unclaim.pre`.
  C. commit: `bl-op: unclaim`, `bl-from-state: claimed`, `bl-to-state: open`.
  D. `unclaim.post`. Delivery plugin: release the code worktree (today's `bl drop` teardown). NOTE: preserving uncommitted deliverable work across a release/takeover is the drop topic's concern, not this one.
  E. merge + cleanup.

review  (claimed → review):
  A. change worktree; stage `state: review`, bump `updated`.
  B. `review.pre`. e.g. a gate plugin may reject if `close requires review`-style policy isn't met — sequence enforcement is a plugin pre concern, not core.
  C. commit: `bl-op: review`, `bl-from-state: claimed`, `bl-to-state: review`.
  D. `review.post` — the DELIVERY step. Delivery plugin operates on the EXISTING code worktree: commit any uncommitted work, merge integration→work/<id> (conflicts surface HERE), squash work/<id>→integration as one feature commit, write `delivered_in`. forge-gated variant: a forge plugin wired here instead pushes work/<id> + opens the PR + creates the gate child, leaving `delivered_in` null. Both are "what's wired into review.post."
  E. merge change worktree into operating/; tear down change worktree. Code worktree PERSISTS (review is not the finish line; close removes it).

FAILURE / ROLLBACK ORDER (note 3, unchanged): on any non-zero plugin exit, roll back prior plugins in REVERSE order FIRST, THEN discard the change worktree. Two consequences specific to delivery:
- If `review.post`'s squash already landed on the integration branch and a LATER post-plugin fails, rollback must un-squash (`git reset` locally; un-pushable in forge mode). Mitigation = ORDERING RULE: the delivery/squash plugin takes the highest `NN-` prefix in `review.post/` so nothing runs after it. Not a new problem (today's balls has it); plugin-ownership just makes the boundary explicit.
- The delivery plugin's `rollback` verb must idempotently undo a worktree creation (claim) or a squash (review).

SCOPE — `close.post` (tear down the code worktree, and whether close ships code / couples to a remote) is DEFERRED to topic bl-close-remote-coupling, which is explicitly open. claim/unclaim/review are fully resolved here; close's plugin behavior is not this topic's to settle.

SEAM CHECKS:
- vs §6 (bl-8452): the delivery plugin is an ordinary sibling binary in `claim.post/`, `review.post/` run-parts dirs; subprocess-uniform; self-describes via `protocol`→{protocol, ops}. No core privilege.
- vs §7/§8 (bl-4997): no return channel relied upon — the worktree path is derived, not returned; the plugin mutates by acting on the project repo, not by printing state. Failure order matches note 3.
- vs note 0: "worktree is core" = the CHANGE worktree; the CODE worktree is the separately-identified deliverable workspace, now plugin-owned. No contradiction.

author: Obstructed
text:
§ ID SCHEME + § bl new (create) — RESOLVED (topics bl-f3e0 + bl-284c, resolved as a unit). Becomes two new §§ on next spec revision. Decided with Model A: **id IS the path, not a field.**

CORE REFRAME — the pre/post commit boundary (bl-4997 §4) IS the id-authority boundary. No "really actually pre" phase is needed:
- pre-invoke: the id is NOT sealed → it is NOT on the wire. A pre-plugin that needs the current id READS it from cwd (the single staged-new tasks/*.md). balls re-derives it from the same place after each plugin. No second copy exists, so "stale id" is structurally impossible, not something balls keeps synced. This is the enforcement of "path or field, not both" applied to the wire.
- post-invoke: the id IS sealed → it is on the wire in `metadata` (the §5 `bl-id` trailer), final, no skew.

INVARIANT — id is IMMUTABLE after create. Reassignment is a create-only capability. During claim/unclaim/review/close the id is fixed and MAY ride the wire safely (nothing reassigns it). This kills the whole "what if a plugin renames mid-claim" class.

§3 AMENDMENT — DROP the `id:` frontmatter field. The id is the filename basename of `tasks/<id>.md`, the SOLE source of truth. One token. Preserves the §1 "inspectable, no hashing" property for the most important files (`git log -- tasks/<id>.md` works, `tasks/` greps by id). Rejected Model B (opaque-uuid filename + id-as-frontmatter-field, zero rename) because it reintroduces hashing for task files and forces an index for `bl show <id>` — never introduce an index unless it is the core basis of the application.

§4 AMENDMENT — id_scheme = `{ prefix, length, alphabet }` ONLY. DROP the `generator` enum. Default `{ prefix: "bl-", length: 4, alphabet: "0123456789abcdef" }` — exactly today's scheme. base balls ships ONE generator (random). Non-default generation (timestamp / sequential / uuid) is a `new/pre/` plugin via the SAME git-mv mechanism as any plugin-assigned id — "custom id generation" and "plugin-assigned id" are one seam, not two. (uuid ≈ random with a long length; sequential needs persistent state AND breaks under §3 delete-on-terminal id reuse — both correctly pushed to plugin territory.)

ID VALIDATION — principle is STRING-SAFETY, not an arbitrary charset. Forbid anything that starts subshells, traverses paths, or needs escaping/quoting: no path separators (`/`), no path traversal (`.` — kills `..` and `.md` confusion), no whitespace, no shell metacharacters/escapes, no leading `-`. Concretely: `^[A-Za-z0-9][A-Za-z0-9_-]*$`. Case-sensitive, but the DEFAULT alphabet is lowercase to sidestep case-insensitive-FS (macOS/Windows) collisions — documented caveat, not engineered around.

COLLISION POLICY (asymmetric, principled):
- balls auto-gen collides with an existing `tasks/<id>.md` → RETRY generation (bounded; exhausted space → error). balls owns its default generator, so it may retry.
- plugin-assigned id collides → ABORT with a clear error, NO retry. An explicit plugin choice is authoritative; balls won't silently mutate it.
- Check happens at phase C (post pre-chain, pre-commit): does `tasks/<final-id>.md` already exist on the balls branch.

REASSIGN-DISCOVERY CONVENTION — a reassigning plugin must NOT hardcode the provisional name (an earlier plugin may already have moved it). It discovers the current id by reading the single staged-new `tasks/*.md`, then `git mv`s from there. This is what makes "last writer wins" (run-parts NN- order, §7) actually compose.

DETERMINISTIC create SEQUENCE (the §8 five-phase shape; CLI verb `bl new`, internal op `create`):
  A. balls generates the default-scheme id (the DEFAULT LAYER — first writer, not a choice it later retracts). Create change worktree `changes/<uuid>/` (uuid-named per bl-4997; NOTHING keys off it). Stage `tasks/<default-id>.md`: frontmatter title, state=default_state(open), created/updated=now, optional parent/deps/tags; body from --body/editor. Uncommitted. No `id:` field (§3 amendment).
  B. `create/pre` plugins, cwd = worktree. id NOT on the wire. A plugin may reject (non-zero → reverse-order rollback + discard worktree) or reassign by `git mv`-ing the single staged ball file (override of the default layer via the filesystem — the universal override mechanism here).
  C. balls validates: exactly one NEW `tasks/*.md` exists; final id = its basename; regex-valid; no collision. Commit with the §5 trailer block: `bl-protocol`, `bl-op: create`, `bl-id: <final>`, `bl-actor`. NO bl-from-state/bl-to-state (create has no prior state).
  D. `create/post` plugins, cwd = worktree. `metadata` carries the final `bl-id`. tracker pushes if wired.
  E. merge change worktree into `operating/`; tear down the worktree.

FAILURE MODES (the bl-284c list, finalized under Model A):
1. Two plugins both reassign → last writer wins, "last" = run-parts NN- order; each reads the current single staged file (filesystem, §7). No arbitration.
2. Plugin assigns an invalid id (regex-fail OR collision with an existing ball) → ABORT at phase C, clear error, no retry.
3. "No plugin claims an id AND no id_scheme can produce one" → CANNOT occur at op time: id_scheme is core and always produces (a default was staged in phase A). Degrades to a CONFIG-VALIDATION error (e.g. empty alphabet / length 0) raised BEFORE staging.
4. A `create/post` plugin fails → roll back prior plugins in reverse, THEN discard the worktree before the phase-E merge. The commit creating `tasks/<id>.md` is discarded with the worktree and never reaches `operating/`; the id frees naturally (it was never announced pre-commit, so nothing in-flight is invalidated).

SEAM CHECKS:
- vs §6 (bl-8452): reassigning plugins are ordinary `create/pre|post/` run-parts binaries; subprocess-uniform; the NN- order is the conflict-ordering mechanism. No core privilege.
- vs §7/§8 (bl-4997): no return channel relied upon — reassignment is a `git mv` in the worktree, never printed; the id is read from the tree (pre) or the metadata trailer (post). Worktree-first lifecycle holds with the id off the pre wire as its only refinement.
- vs §3: the `id:` frontmatter field is dropped; filename is sole truth.

author: orionriver@gmail.com
text:
§ TERMINAL OPS (close / drop) — RESOLVED (topic bl-dd3e). Becomes a new § on next spec revision. SUPERSEDES the §3 corrections-note line "Cf. bl-close-remote-coupling — whether close also ships code is open" — that question is now closed (see below).

CORE PRINCIPLE — NO WORKFLOW ENFORCEMENT IN CORE. All sequence/workflow enforcement is EITHER a plugin pre-invoke (best-effort, can fail to reject) OR skill hinting — never a core check. Closing a never-claimed ball, closing from the wrong cwd, closing out-of-order: not balls' job to stop. Destination semantics, not source (consistent with the §3 "only hardcoded rejection is claim-on-already-claimed" rule). This makes close/drop maximally simple.

WHAT CORE close/drop DO (identical mechanics; differ only in intent + bl-to-state):
- Stage a DELETION of tasks/<id>.md in the change worktree (changes/<uuid>/), §8 worktree-first lifecycle like any op.
- §5 trailer block: bl-op: close|drop, bl-from-state: <prev>, bl-to-state: closed|dropped.
- Tear down the CHANGE worktree (phase E). That is the ONLY worktree core owns — the "worktree is core, so close/drop own teardown" line refers to the ephemeral change worktree, NOT the deliverable code worktree.
- Recovery: git log --diff-filter=D -- tasks/<id>.md; bl show <id> reconstructs from history.

WHAT CORE close DOES NOT DO — ships no code, pushes no project remote, runs no source-state check:
- CODE delivery is already done by the delivery/worktree plugin's review.post (squash work/<id>→integration). close ADDS no re-ship. The review→close split SURVIVES. close.post is teardown of the code worktree (delivery plugin) — see bl-2a77.
- The TRACKER plugin may push on close, but ONLY the balls-state branches (the ball-deletion commit) — never the project code branch. "close is related to remotes" resolves to exactly this: tracker pushes the balls branch; core does not.
- Whether the delivery plugin's close.post ALSO moves project code / pushes the project remote is a FORGE/MERGE-PROCESS question with no universal answer (merge-and-push vs PR-gated vs always-push-back). balls makes NO authoritative decision here — it is per-user plugin config (the two delivery variants in bl-2a77). Closes bl-close-remote-coupling.

drop-from-open (never-claimed): no claim ⇒ delivery plugin's claim.post never ran ⇒ no code worktree ⇒ drop.post is a no-op by idempotency. Not a special case — falls out of idempotency.

author: mark
text:
§ DELIVERY/WORKTREE PLUGIN CONTRACT — RESOLVED (topic bl-2a77). Becomes a new § on next spec revision. Completes the bl-d66e "§ STATE TRANSITIONS" deferral: bl-d66e drew the boundary (core flips ball state on `balls`; a SIBLING plugin owns everything touching the project repo) but left the plugin's own contract — close.post teardown, the exact derived-path function, cross-op state, rollback, the two delivery variants, delivered_in ownership — unspecced. This § specs it. Settled inputs carried, not re-litigated: two-cwd reality; `binding.invocation_path` on the §7 wire; worktree path is DERIVED not returned; sibling-not-tracker (local-worktrees-without-remote ⊥ remote-without-worktrees).

IDENTITY. One responsibility: own the deliverable CODE worktree — a `git worktree` of the PROJECT repo on branch `work/<id>` — end to end across claim→review→close. A sibling of tracker, default-wired alongside it but separate. Self-describes via `<plugin> protocol` → {protocol, ops} like any §6 binary; no core privilege. Base balls never opens the project repo, so "nothing on main / nothing in the project tree" is STRUCTURAL: only this plugin ever commits to the integration branch, deliberately, on review.post.

DERIVED-PATH FUNCTION (resolves bl-d66e mechanism-cost 3 concretely). The code-worktree path is a PURE FUNCTION of (binding, id):
  worktree_path(binding, id) = $XDG_STATE_HOME/balls/plugins/<plugin-name>/<pct-enc(binding.invocation_path)>/<id>/
balls computes+prints it (preserving today's "claim prints the path" UX); the plugin independently creates it at the same location. No return channel. `<id>` comes from the §5 `bl-id` metadata trailer on the post-invoke wire (Model A: id is immutable after create — see § ID SCHEME note — so `work/<id>` cannot drift mid-lifecycle). The branch name `work/<id>` is the second derived token. Both path and branch are pure (binding, id); nothing else feeds them. The worktree lives in the plugin's XDG TERRITORY, not the project tree — keeps the project working directory pristine (the worktree extends "nothing on main" to "nothing in the project checkout"); its `.git` file points back at the project repo's `.git`, found via `binding.invocation_path`.

ELEGANT CONSEQUENCE — THE PLUGIN IS STATELESS ACROSS OPS (subtracts bl-d66e mechanism-cost 4). Because the worktree path AND branch are pure functions of (binding, id), and (binding, id) ride every transition op's wire, the plugin needs NO id-keyed scratch to remember its resource between claim/review/close. It RECOMPUTES the path each op and checks the filesystem. Every hook is therefore idempotent by construction:
  - claim.post  = create-if-absent  (path exists? no-op : `git -C <project> worktree add <path> -b work/<id>`)
  - review.post = deliver-the-existing (operate on the worktree at <path>)
  - close.post  = remove-if-present  (`git -C <project> worktree remove <path>` + delete branch)
COORDINATION WITH bl-b0b6 (rollback/scratch): bl-b0b6 is choosing an id-keyed scratch-under-`plugins/<name>/` convention for plugins whose cross-op state ISN'T derivable. The delivery plugin is the case that DISSOLVES rather than uses it — its state is fully derived, so it stores nothing. bl-b0b6's convention serves plugins that genuinely can't derive (e.g. a forge plugin caching a PR number — and even that lands in `task.external.<plugin>` on the ball file, not loose scratch). Pin: derive before you store; the delivery plugin is the worked example.

THE FOUR HOOKS.

claim.post (open→claimed): materialize. `git -C <project> worktree add <derived-path> -b work/<id>`, create-if-absent. cwd is the CHANGE worktree (balls-state, XDG); the plugin deliberately ignores it and acts on the project repo via `binding.invocation_path` — same convention-break as tracker acting on a remote (bl-d66e mechanism-cost 1).

unclaim.post / drop.post (→open / terminal): release the code worktree (`git worktree remove` + delete `work/<id>`). drop-from-`open` (never-claimed) = no-op by idempotency (claim.post never ran ⇒ no worktree ⇒ remove-if-present is a no-op). Not a special case; falls out of the derived-path existence check. PRESERVING uncommitted deliverable work across a release/takeover (the orphan-claim concern) is the drop topic's business, not this contract's — this hook just releases.

review.post (claimed→review) — THE DELIVERY STEP, and the seam where the two variants diverge (see below). Operates on the EXISTING code worktree: commit any uncommitted work, merge integration→work/<id> (conflicts surface HERE, not at close), then either squash (direct) or push+gate (forge). Sorts LAST (highest `NN-` prefix) in `review.post/` — see ROLLBACK.

close.post (review→closed) — TEARDOWN (the bl-d66e deferral, now resolved; consistent with bl-dd3e "§ TERMINAL OPS"). Core close ships NO code and enforces nothing (bl-dd3e); close.post is purely the delivery plugin tearing down its own resource: `git -C <project> worktree remove <derived-path>` + delete `work/<id>`. The review→close split SURVIVES — review.post already delivered (direct) or handed to the forge (forge); close.post does NOT re-ship. Whether close.post ALSO pushes the project remote is the variant's config (forge variant may, direct variant doesn't), never a core call — exactly the bl-dd3e resolution.

CWD-SAFETY GUARD (carry-forward from bl-dd3e, note 2 on this ball, now placed). The inviolable "never `bl close` from inside the worktree being torn down" rule lives HERE, as close.post's first action: if cwd is inside <derived-path>, the plugin refuses/aborts teardown. It is PLUGIN BEST-EFFORT, not a core guarantee — core close has no cwd guard (bl-dd3e); a plugin can fail to reject; equally valid to leave it as skill hinting. It belongs to the delivery plugin because the change worktree (which core owns) is never where the user stands — they stand in the CODE worktree, which only this plugin knows about.

TWO VARIANTS AS TWO PLUGINS (port of bl-00ca/bl-ac91/bl-b017; "what's wired into review.post" is the only difference). The CURRENT-balls forge work is the reference impl — port semantics, don't reinvent.

  DIRECT (default, local-squash) — today's trunk-based flow. review.post: squash `work/<id>`→integration as one feature commit whose SUBJECT carries the `[bl-id]` delivery tag (the plugin's analog of core's §5 trailer — this tag is delivery GROUND TRUTH). Worktree persists until close. close.post: tear down. delivered_in: see REFRAME. Integration branch resolves to `task.target_branch ?? HEAD@<project-repo>` (the forge-SPEC resolution chain; repo-level target_branch was already dropped there).

  FORGE-GATED (opt-in) — external review (PR/MR) produces the merge commit. review.post does NOT squash and does NOT touch the integration branch: commit WIP, merge integration→work/<id> (conflicts still surface here), PUSH `work/<id>` to the remote, create a GATE CHILD ("Forge: PR merged for <id>", tag `forge-gate`) linked `gates` from the parent, print a PR-title hint containing `[bl-id]`. The forge merge later carries `[bl-id]` into the integration branch. A companion forge plugin's `sync` polls PR state and closes the gate child on merge (carrying the merge SHA); it never closes the parent — operator/automation does.
  GREENFIELD TRANSLATION OF THE GATE (load-bearing — the forge SPEC leaned on core's gates check, which greenfield CORE NO LONGER HAS: bl-dd3e removed all close enforcement). So the forge variant must SUPPLY ITS OWN `close.pre` gate-enforcement plugin that refuses close while an open `gates` child exists. The forge SPEC's BC win ("old bl refuses close via the existing gates check") becomes, in greenfield, "the forge delivery bundle wires a close.pre check" — sequence enforcement is a plugin pre concern (the §3 / bl-dd3e principle), not core. This is the one real porting delta; everything else carries verbatim.

delivered_in — REFRAME (dissolves the "who writes it / who leaves it null" scope item). Stay true to § ID SCHEME's rule (never introduce an index/cache unless it's the core basis of the app; "id IS the path, not a field"): the analog is "DELIVERY IS THE TAG, NOT A FIELD." The `[bl-id]` tag in the integration-branch commit subject is already ground truth (forge SPEC principle 5); a stored `delivered_in` is a redundant cache of it. So in greenfield delivered_in is NOT a written field — it is a DERIVED QUERY the delivery plugin answers by tag-scan (`git log --grep [bl-id]`) on the resolved integration branch. This collapses today's stored-hint+tag-scan-fallback to JUST the tag-scan, and dissolves the variant asymmetry: there is nothing to "write" (direct) or "leave null" (forge) — the query simply reports "not yet on the integration branch" for an un-merged forge task and the SHA once merged, in BOTH variants, with no staleness. The cross-clone case (a closing clone lacking the integration branch ref) is handled honestly: the tracker fetches the ref, or the query reports "unknown from here" — never a stale cache. NOTE the deletion interaction: close deletes `tasks/<id>.md` (bl-dd3e), so a stored field would survive only in history anyway; the tag on the integration branch is the durable record by design. (If a future need for an offline hint appears, re-add it as a delivery-plugin-owned OPTIONAL field — never core, since core knows nothing about code delivery — written by direct's review.post; but default to the derived query.)

ROLLBACK VERB + ORDERING (specifics; general rule deferred to bl-b0b6). On any non-zero plugin exit, balls rolls prior plugins back in REVERSE order FIRST, then discards the CHANGE worktree (note 3, unchanged). The delivery plugin adds a THIRD side-effect tier between bl-b0b6's two (change-worktree file changes auto-undone by discard / external services best-effort): a COMMIT ON THE PROJECT REPO that discarding the change worktree does NOT undo. Its `rollback` op, by phase:
  - rollback claim.post → `git worktree remove` the just-created code worktree + delete `work/<id>` (idempotent: absent ⇒ no-op).
  - rollback review.post, DIRECT → un-squash: `git reset --hard HEAD~1` on the integration branch (local, always reversible — nothing was pushed).
  - rollback review.post, FORGE → the push is the un-undoable external side-effect; best-effort delete the remote `work/<id>` + drop the gate child (a re-entrant `bl drop`, depth-suppressed per bl-8452). Exit code ignored, load-bearing facts to stderr (bl-b0b6 best-effort contract).
ORDERING RULE (generalize as a contract recommendation): a plugin with an un-undoable side-effect SORTS LAST in its phase so nothing runs after it — making its rollback the rare path. The delivery/squash plugin therefore takes the highest `NN-` in `review.post/`. Not a new problem (today's balls has the same un-squash hazard); plugin-ownership just makes the boundary explicit.

SEAM CHECKS.
  - vs §6 (bl-8452 dispatcher): ordinary sibling binaries in `claim.post/`, `review.post/`, `close.post/` (and forge's `close.pre/`) run-parts dirs; subprocess-uniform; the `NN-` order IS the ordering mechanism. No core path.
  - vs §7/§8 (bl-4997 worktree-first / no return channel): nothing returned — worktree path is derived, branch is derived, the plugin mutates by acting on the project repo, never by printing state. Stdout is diagnostics/hints only. Failure order matches note 3.
  - vs § ID SCHEME (bl-f3e0+bl-284c, Model A): `<id>` is the immutable `bl-id` trailer; `work/<id>` and the worktree path are sealed at claim and stable through close (note 1 on this ball). The delivery plugin is a TRANSITION-op plugin — id sealed, on the wire — never a create/pre reassigner, so it never needs the staged-tree id-discovery convention.
  - vs § TERMINAL OPS (bl-dd3e): core close/drop delete the ball file with zero enforcement; the code-worktree teardown + cwd guard + delivered_in resolution are ALL this plugin's close.post, never core. The review→close split survives.
  - vs § STATE TRANSITIONS (bl-d66e): this § is the completion of that note's deferral; the claim/unclaim/review sequences there are unchanged — this adds close.post, the derived-path function body, the stateless-across-ops result, the two variants in full, delivered_in, and rollback.

author: mark
text:
§ DROP THE `review` VERB — CLOSE-CENTRIC DELIVERY — RESOLVED (topic bl-03fd). Becomes a § revision on next spec revision. SUPERSEDES: the bl-2a77 "§ DELIVERY/WORKTREE PLUGIN" line placing the squash on `review.post`, and the bl-d66e "§ STATE TRANSITIONS" `review (claimed→review)` sequence. Confirmed with the user 2026-06-02.

WHY review dies. The deliverable's life is: get a workspace (claim) → write code (no verb) → DELIVER (squash/merge to integration) → RETIRE (delete ball + tear down worktree). In the self-merge default (one agent takes its own task all the way), DELIVER and RETIRE are one uninterrupted act. `review` was only ever the GAP between them — and in the pure default that gap is empty. A forge gate / merge queue / human approval is precisely "something that holds the task open in that gap." So `review` is not a step the agent takes; it is the NAME of a waystation a gate plugin parks the task in. Modeling it as a core verb makes every self-merge agent type `bl review && bl close` where the first is ceremony. Consistent with bl-dd3e (no workflow enforcement in core; states are open strings; destination semantics) and bl-4778 (states-as-config) and the "new verbs are a smell" principle.

NEW CORE DELIVERABLE VERBS: `claim`, `close` (+ `unclaim`, `drop`). NO `review`. Delivery and teardown are the two halves of close, split across the §8 commit boundary:
- close.pre → delivery plugin squashes `work/<id>`→integration (conflicts surface HERE; subject carries the `[bl-id]` delivery tag). PRE placement is STRICTLY BETTER than bl-2a77's review.post: a failed merge aborts BEFORE the ball-deletion commits, so a failed delivery automatically leaves the task alive. Sorts LAST (highest `NN-`) so its un-squash rollback is the rare path.
- core deletes `tasks/<id>.md` (§5 `bl-op: close` trailer).
- close.post → delivery plugin tears down the code worktree (+ the cwd-safety guard from bl-2a77).
This REVISES the bl-2a77 four-hook set: it becomes claim.post (materialize) / unclaim+drop.post (release) / close.pre (deliver) / close.post (teardown). The plugin's stateless-across-ops + derived-path results from bl-2a77 carry unchanged.

GATES REPLACE review (no verb, no `review` state):
- SYNCHRONOUS gate = a `close.pre` COMMAND that runs and rejects on non-zero. Build/test is exactly this — it is the forge SPEC's old `review.gate_command` re-homed onto close.pre, no review verb, no child task.
- ASYNCHRONOUS gate = a materialized gate CHILD (created by `create.post`, tag `gate`) resolved by an EXTERNAL actor (forge PR merge / merge-queue land / human approval). `close.pre` refuses while it is open; the external actor closes the child; `bl close` again succeeds (delivery already done by the forge → close.post just tears down).
"Awaiting merge/approval" = "has an open gate child" (an existing structural signal), NOT a distinct `review` status string. A plugin MAY set a status string for `bl list` readability (states-as-config), but core needs none. The two-agent submit→approve flow falls out of the async gate: A's `bl close` opens an approval gate and blocks; the reviewer closes it; A or B closes the task.

FORGE BC ARGUMENT DISSOLVES. The forge SPEC's backwards-compat win ("an old `bl` refuses close via the EXISTING core gates check") was an artifact of CORE-enforced gates. Greenfield core enforces nothing (bl-dd3e), so the gate is plugin all the way down. This is fine: the default install ships the workflow plugin wired in (gate enforcement is default-via-config, naked-core has none) — the same shape as tracker/federation being shipped-plugin defaults.

----

§ PLUGIN-INVOCATION LIFECYCLE (DRAFT for final repo docs; folded into the working doc per user request 2026-06-02). This is the authoritative per-op sequence, verb-agnostic. The happy-path spine (user's 6 steps) plus the two parts a half-spec would omit (integrate+teardown, and rollback):

1. balls MAKES THE PLACE TO WORK — the change worktree on the terminus branch. For `init`, it makes the anchor repo instead (the substrate-creation phase; per bl-6406 `init` is a NORMAL op, not a dispatch exception — it just creates the anchor where other ops create a worktree).
2. balls SIGNALS EACH `pre` PLUGIN IN ORDER — the §7 wire: current state + WHAT IT PLANS TO DO.
3. PLUGINS MODIFY THE ENVIRONMENT — by editing the worktree (e.g. a create/pre plugin renames the ball file to assign an id). No return channel (bl-4997); stdout is diagnostics.
4. balls APPLIES THE INTENDED CHANGE AND COMMITS — with the §5 trailer block. THIS COMMIT IS THE pre/post BOUNDARY.
5. balls SIGNALS EACH `post` PLUGIN IN ORDER — WHAT HAPPENED, with the sealed `bl-id`/state on the wire.
6. PLUGINS REACT — tracker pushes; the delivery plugin acts on the project repo.

Beyond the 6 happy-path steps (omitting either makes it a half-spec):
- INTEGRATE + TEARDOWN (current §8 phase E): after step 6, balls merges the change worktree into `operating/` and tears it down. The committed change becomes real here.
- FAILURE / ROLLBACK (note 3): any non-zero `pre`/`post` exit → roll prior plugins back in REVERSE order FIRST, then discard the change worktree. The change worktree's file changes are auto-undone by the discard; a plugin's EXTERNAL or project-repo side effects are the plugin's `rollback` op's job (bl-b0b6).

SEAM: this lifecycle is the frame `claim`/`close`/`create`/`init`/`drop` all instantiate — the close-centric delivery above is just "what the delivery + gate plugins do in close's pre/post steps." No op is special; `init`'s only difference is step 1 builds the anchor.

author: mark
text:
§ TASK TAXONOMY + BLOCKER MODEL — RESOLVED (topic bl-a408). Becomes a § revision + a §3 (ball schema) amendment on next spec revision. Converged with the user 2026-06-02 over several rounds. REVISES the link taxonomy (parent/deps/gates → parent + blockers) and REFINES the bl-2a77/bl-03fd delivery-plugin to KIND-BLIND.

ONE NODE TYPE. There is only `task`. `epic`/`issue`/`gate` are TAGS (free strings, zero behavior). The `task_type` field (today's `-t`, where only `epic` had special rendering) loses all behavioral meaning — it is a label. "Epic rendering" becomes the emergent "this task has children." No `unclaimable` bit anywhere — which is what kills the epic-claim churn at the root (agents no longer attempt-and-fail to claim epics; epics simply aren't `ready`, see below).

THE BLOCKER PRIMITIVE (the one relational mechanism; subsumes both deps and gates).
A blocker is a property of the BLOCKED task naming WHICH OF ITS OWN TRANSITIONS is gated and pointing at the task that must resolve first:
    blockers: [ { id: bl-0000, on: close } ]
= "this task cannot CLOSE until bl-0000 is closed-or-dropped." `on` ∈ {claim, close} — the OP whose `.pre` enforces it. The `.pre` is IMPLICIT: blocking is always a pre-op rejection (you cannot block at `.post`; the op already happened), so `on` carries the op, not `close.pre`. "Resolved" = the blocker is closed OR dropped (dropping a subtask correctly unblocks its epic).

EMERGENT CONCEPTS (no types, all fall out of {blocker, on}):
- DEPENDENCY = a claim-blocker. (Can't START until it resolves.)
- GATE = a close-blocker. (Can't FINISH until it resolves.) Build/test/approval/forge all are this.
- EPIC = a task whose children are its claim-blockers. It's not `ready` while subtasks are open; the moment they close it becomes ready (claim it to do closeout, or a plugin auto-closes — auto-close is plugin, default off). "epic IS having claim-blocking children," not a type.

READINESS / CLOSEABILITY (the churn fix, type-free):
- ready(A) = A is open + unclaimed + every CLAIM-blocker resolved. NOTE: a gate child is an open child that does NOT affect readiness (it's a CLOSE-blocker) — this is why "no open children" was the wrong definition.
- closeable(A) = every CLOSE-blocker resolved; checked at A's `close.pre`.

DEADLOCK AVOIDANCE (the gate-child ↔ parent puzzle). Wire exactly ONE edge: the gate child close-blocks the parent (`parent.blockers += {child, close}`); the parent does NOT claim-block the child. So the gate is freely claimable/closeable; the reciprocal edge that would deadlock is simply never created. Two things make leaving "do the gate after the parent's work" UNENFORCED safe (and enforcing it IS the deadlock): (1) the gate-check runs against the parent's pre-delivery WORK BRANCH (which exists once the parent is claimed), so nothing needs the parent to be formally "done" → no `review` window is resurrected; (2) running a gate early just fails/wastes, never deadlocks. Ordering is skill habit ("resolve gates last"), not a rule. Circular blockers generally: NOT gated by code (links are mutable — unlink to fix); `bl doctor` (bl-e8a5) may surface them.

PLUGIN SPLIT (each default-shipped, each ⊥, coordinating ONLY through core schema — never by sniffing each other's tags):
- CORE: stores schema (`parent` tree pointer + `blockers:[{id,on}]` + `state` + free `tags`) and the lifecycle hooks. ENFORCES NOTHING. It MAY READ blockers to answer `ready`/`closeable` as a hint, but a blocked task is still claimable/closeable in naked core — enforcement is the gating plugin's alone.
- GATING PLUGIN (default): the SOLE enforcer. `claim.pre` rejects on an open claim-blocker; `close.pre` rejects on an open close-blocker. Tiny. Contributes NO CLI.
- DELIVERY/WORKTREE PLUGIN (default, SEPARATE — refines bl-2a77/bl-03fd): KIND-BLIND. Makes a worktree on `claim.post`, delivers-if-changes + tears down on close, and NEVER branches on task kind. Non-deliverables (epic/gate) are normally CLOSED WITHOUT BEING CLAIMED → no worktree is ever made; a claimed non-deliverable gets a harmless EMPTY worktree (the `close.pre` squash is a no-op when `work/<id>` has no changes, `close.post` remove is a no-op when the path is absent — both already idempotent/derived in bl-2a77). This DISSOLVES the cross-plugin tag-coordination problem: the worktree plugin reads NONE of the gating plugin's tags. (If empty worktrees ever annoy: one generic core field `deliverable: false` the worktree plugin reads — NOT a tag vocabulary. Skip it by default.) Preserves worktrees-without-gating ⊥ gating-without-worktrees ⊥ tracker.
- TRACKER (default): remote, unchanged.
The expanding plugin space is fine: the problem was never plugin COUNT, it's plugin COUPLING — and kind-blind worktrees + blocker-as-core-schema decouple them, so the inter-plugin contract is the small fixed core schema, not pairwise tag agreements.

EDGE STORAGE — on the BLOCKED task ("the task knows it's blocked"): `ready`/`closeable` are O(1) local reads, no tree-walking. Consequence accepted: `bl create --parent E` must EDIT E (append the new child to E's claim-blockers) — a write to the parent, fine because all balls writes canonicalize through the change worktree, which serializes/merges the concurrent case. `parent` still lives on the child for the tree; that field is scanned only for DISPLAY (`bl show` tree), where walking is acceptable. (`parent` and the claim-blocker edge coincide for a subtask but mean different things — containment vs ordering — so they are not redundant in general.)

CLI FRONT-DOORS — CORE (they write CORE schema; plugins contribute NO CLI — plugins are hook binaries per bl-8452, they don't extend `bl`'s parser, so there is no `--help` composition and no plugin-injected flags). Naked core accepts them and writes the edges; they're simply UNENFORCED without the gating plugin.
- `--parent E`  → child `parent: E` + `E.blockers += {child, claim}`   (subtask)
- `--gates X`   → child `parent: X` + `X.blockers += {child, close}`   (gate)
- `--needs B`   → `self.blockers += {B, claim}`                         (cross-tree dependency)
General form (the rare "block any task at any op") writes `blockers` directly; the three flags are the directly-supported front doors for the only two-and-a-half real use-cases.

GATES ARE TASKS ONLY (no command-check primitive). Every gate-check is "is task X closed?". Build/test = a build-gate CHILD a build plugin creates on `create.post` and closes on pass; forge gate = a child a forge `sync` closes on merge; approval = a child a human closes. The RESOLUTION MECHANISM (manual / build-plugin / forge-sync) is pluggable; the BLOCKING mechanism is one thing. "All issues get a build gate" = a build plugin's `create.post` adds the child per issue (machine-managed; the per-issue file is the uniformity price, accepted).

§3 AMENDMENT — drop `task_type`-as-behavior (free label only); add `blockers: [{id, on}]` (default empty); `parent` stays a core read-only pointer. No new `Status`/state for any of this. SUPERSEDES the old parent/deps/gates link taxonomy: deps and gates were never two relations — they are one `blockers` edge parameterized by `on`.

OPEN DETAIL (minor, flagged): a subtask claim-blocks its parent (per user's model). That leaves a rare gap — add a subtask to an ALREADY-CLAIMED epic, then close the epic with the child open. Leaning: skill says "don't add subtasks to a claimed epic," rather than adding a second close-block edge per subtask. Revisit if it bites.

SEAM CHECKS:
- vs bl-dd3e (no core enforcement): blockers are core SCHEMA (data), enforcement is the gating plugin — consistent; `ready`/`closeable` are reads/hints, not policy.
- vs bl-2a77/bl-03fd (delivery/worktree): refined to kind-blind; the close-centric lifecycle (deliver→close.pre, teardown→close.post) and gates-replace-review hold — gates are now `close.pre` blocker-checks the gating plugin enforces, before the delivery plugin's squash (highest `NN-`).
- vs bl-8452 (dispatcher): plugins add no CLI; front-door flags are core. The gating + delivery plugins are ordinary hook binaries in `claim.pre`/`close.pre`/`claim.post`/`close.post`.
- vs § ID SCHEME (Model A): `blockers` reference immutable ids (the `bl-id` filename basename); no skew.

author: mark
text:
§ FEDERATION — TRAIL TOPOLOGY + CONFIG LAYERING — RESOLVED (topic bl-62bc). Becomes a § revision on next spec revision. Completes the bl-6406 (§9) deferral "Trail topology + config layering for multi-step federation: bl-62bc." Builds on settled vocab: anchor → step(s) → terminus, walked by each branch's next-step pointer, stop-on-revisit, no depth cap (bl-6406 retired the epic's "one hop").

THE CRUX — TWO DISTINCT MULTI-CLONE MODELS, do not conflate (this is what "trail topology" resolves):
  • SHARED HUB (--remote / master_url, the EXISTING multi-repo model): ONE `balls` branch, N clones MIRROR it via a remote. Same tasks because it is literally the same branch. This is NOT a trail — anchor==terminus==operating locally, tracker just push/pulls that one branch. No step.
  • FEDERATION (--center, THIS topic): your anchor is a SEPARATE local `balls` branch; you SHADOW someone else's branch as a down-trail layer. A trail with ≥1 step. Config layers (yours over theirs); tasks resolve from the terminus (the center). You keep your own anchor (local config + onward pointer) but read/write a shared task store at the center.
  Federation exists for "I want my own local config/anchor overrides but read a SHARED task store + inherit shared config." Mirroring (--remote) and shadowing (--center) are orthogonal and may compose (rare): mirror your anchor to your own remote AND shadow a center.

Q1 — NEXT-STEP POINTER SCHEMA. Minimal: a single scalar `next: <url>`. Lives in tracker territory on the anchor's balls branch (`config/plugins/tracker/remote.yaml`) — committed and PORTABLE (a fresh clone inherits the federation intent), and core never reads the URL (full-symmetry: only tracker knows it is a remote).
  REJECTED additions (each fails a project discipline test):
   • branch — not a field. Branch is the resolved `branch` config (§4 default `balls`), uniform along the trail. A per-step override, if ever genuinely needed, rides as a ref-fragment `<url>#<branch>`, not a new key. ("New fields are a smell.")
   • auth hints — not a field. git's credential machinery (ssh config, helpers, URL scheme) owns auth; duplicating it fails the footprint + knob-earns-its-keep tests. Tracker just `git fetch <url>`.
   • ttl / refresh interval — not a field. Refresh cadence is `bl sync`'s concern, not stored state (see Q4). N independent per-step timers is exactly the mechanism to avoid.
  The whole schema is one scalar. Resist growing it.

Q2 — CONFIG LAYERING ALONG THE TRAIL. A multi-step trail composes BOTH §-rules, each by its OWN rule, independently — they coexist because they are different KINDS (value-merge vs identity-select):
  • config/balls.yaml (scalar/object/list fields) → §4 innermost-wins. Trail order = innermost(ANCHOR) → step → … → outermost(TERMINUS). Your anchor SHADOWS the center — "shadow theirs underneath yours" (§0). SUPERSEDES §4's "(only if … points at one; one hop)": no depth cap; layer across the WHOLE trail. List fields still compose via `_prepend`/`_append`/`_ban` per layer; scalars/objects replace.
  • run-parts `config/plugins/<op>/<phase>/` dirs → first-defining-layer-wins, WHOLE, no merge (§9/bl-6406). Walking innermost→outermost, the FIRST trail layer that defines a given `<op>/<phase>/` dir wins entirely; outer versions of THAT dir are not merged. Different ops resolve independently.
  N>2 is just the length-N generalization of the two-layer (anchor/center) case §4 already sketched; the rules were defined for N=2 and apply unchanged. The walk that produces the layer ordering = the trail walk (each branch's own `next:` chains to the next, stop-on-revisit).

Q3 — `--center <url>` + POST-INIT EDITING. CONFIRMED (already in the bl-6406 EDGE-CASE MATRIX). `--center <url>` at `bl init` = tracker writing the next-step pointer (`config/plugins/tracker/remote.yaml: next: <url>`) on the anchor; trail = anchor → center. After init, federation is changed by editing that committed pointer file and running `bl sync` (tracker re-walks the trail, fetches/prunes step checkouts, re-points operating/). NO separate "federate" verb — `--center` is init-time sugar for the pointer; the durable control surface is the committed pointer + `bl sync` ("new verbs are a smell"). `--center`/`--remote` also OVERRIDE a stealth lock (bl-6406: explicit onward-routing is deliberate). Role split (faithful to bl-6406's `--remote > --center > XDG > origin` chain): `--remote` sets tracker's own sync remote for your anchor (mirror); `--center` writes the onward pointer (shadow). When only `--center` is given it also serves as the trail terminus (pure-satellite — your tasks resolve to the center).

Q4 — TRACKER MAINTAINS DOWNSTREAM STEP CHECKOUTS + REFRESH. Each step on the trail gets a parallel checkout at `plugins/tracker/<pct-enc(step-url)>/` (the §1 layout; two repos sharing a URL share one checkout). Tracker materializes them on init/sync/prime by walking the committed `next:` chain. REFRESH SCOPE: `bl sync` re-walks the trail and fetches/fast-forwards EVERY step checkout (terminus + all intermediates) — a config change anywhere up-trail changes your effective config, and a task change at the terminus changes what you read, so the refresh UNIT is the whole trail per sync, not N independent ttls (this is why Q1 rejects a ttl). `bl prime` syncs as part of session bootstrap. Stop-on-revisit (literal-URL dedup) bounds the walk: a cyclic/misconfigured trail terminates at the revisit and each distinct URL is fetched once.
  DEFERRED to bl-375a (sync/prime walkthrough — adjacent open sibling): the fast-forward-vs-abort contention semantics, and whether `prime` calls `sync` or they are peers. This topic fixes only that the refresh SCOPE is the entire trail and the cadence is sync-driven (not pointer-stored). Seam flagged to bl-375a.

Q5 — READ PATHS. CONFIRMED, with the precise split (the LOAD-BEARING asymmetry that makes federation "shadow config, not merge tasks"):
  • config/balls.yaml — FEDERATES (layers the whole trail, §4 innermost-wins, no depth cap; Q2).
  • tasks/<id>.md — does NOT federate; TERMINUS-LOCAL. Exactly ONE task store on a trail: the terminus's tasks/. operating/ resolves to the terminus, so all reads (`bl list`/`bl show`) and writes (claim/close) hit the terminus tasks/. The anchor and intermediate steps contribute ZERO tasks to the view — config only. If steps' tasks merged you'd have N stores to reconcile + id collisions + distributed writes — explicitly avoided. One store, many config overlays.
  • config/plugins/tracker/remote.yaml (the onward pointer) is read per-branch as the walk proceeds (each branch states where IT goes next) — it is NOT layered/merged down-trail; you don't merge onward pointers. The run-parts `<op>/<phase>/` dirs under config/plugins/ DO resolve down-trail (first-defining-wins, Q2).

SEAM (core vs tracker) — full symmetry preserved. CORE owns the trail abstraction (anchor/step/terminus/operating) and the §4 config-layer merge over LOCAL checkouts; it follows next-step pointers, reads each visited branch's config/balls.yaml as a layer, treats the terminus tasks/ as the store. Core does NOT know a step is a remote. TRACKER owns remote↔local translation: on init/sync/prime it fetches each committed `next:` URL into `plugins/tracker/<pct-enc(url)>/` (the §1 layout) and ensures operating/ → terminus. The committed `next: <url>` is the portable federation INTENT; the realized local trail (checkouts) is what core walks. STEALTH = no pointer, trail length 1, anchor==terminus==operating, tracker fetches nothing — identical walk/merge code for N=1 and N>1; "federated" is just "tracker populated some step checkouts."

DEFERRED / SEAM REFERENCES (kept faithful to topic boundaries):
  • Physical anchor-vs-operating realization in tracker mode (real dir vs symlink, where the anchor checkout physically sits when operating/ symlinks to the terminus): §1 / workspace-layout — already bl-6406-deferred there; this topic does not re-open it.
  • sync contention + prime/sync relationship: bl-375a.

author: Unfairly
text: §3/§7/§10 ADDENDUM — status derivation is SINGLE-SOURCE (bl-4778 follow-up). The 3-state ladder (claimant set ⇒ claimed [claim-blockers not evaluated]; else unresolved claim-blocker ⇒ blocked; else ready) must live in exactly ONE core function. `bl list`/`bl show` render by CALLING it; status-mirroring plugins (jira/github) must NOT re-implement it — core surfaces the derived status to plugins (a `bl status <id>` read, or a derived field on the §7 post wire) so a plugin consumes the value, never re-walks claimant/blockers. Single-source-of-truth applies to the DERIVATION, not just the stored data: one ladder, many readers; N consumers re-deriving = N drift points. Governs bl-70f5 (§10 ready/blocked is where the ladder naturally lives), bl-5d56 (§7 wire exposes it), bl-dfbd + the display path (consume, don't recompute).

Revises the bl-a408 'gating plugin is the SOLE enforcer' decision — driven by bl-70f5 implementation: forge/build gates would otherwise depend on the gating plugin being installed (plugin-on-plugin dependency), and core already owns the predicates.
